title: Indefinite article: a vs an
rule: Use 'a' before a consonant SOUND and 'an' before a vowel SOUND. It depends on pronunciation, not spelling: 'an hour' (silent h), 'a university' (starts with a /juː/ sound).

title: Definite article with unique/known nouns
rule: Use 'the' when both speaker and listener know which specific thing is meant, or when it is unique (the sun, the government). Omit articles with most uncountable and plural nouns used in a general sense.

title: Subject-verb agreement, present simple
rule: In the present simple, add -s/-es to the verb for third person singular (he/she/it). Plural and I/you/we/they take the base form.

title: Agreement with collective and indefinite subjects
rule: Indefinite pronouns like everyone, someone, each, either take a singular verb. Collective nouns (team, family) take singular in American usage.
examples: Each of the students has a book.

title: Present simple vs present continuous
rule: Use present simple for habits and general truths; use present continuous (be + -ing) for actions happening now or around now. Stative verbs (know, want, believe) are normally not used in the continuous.
examples: I know the answer. (not: I am knowing)

title: Past simple vs present perfect
rule: Use past simple with a finished time (yesterday, in 2020). Use present perfect for past events with present relevance or an unfinished time period (this week, ever, already).
examples: Have you ever been to Rome?

title: Prepositions of time: in/on/at
rule: Use 'at' for clock times and points (at 6pm, at night), 'on' for days and dates (on Monday, on 5 May), 'in' for longer periods (in July, in 2026, in the morning).

title: Dependent prepositions after verbs/adjectives
rule: Many verbs and adjectives require a fixed preposition: depend ON, interested IN, good AT, afraid OF, listen TO. These are learned as chunks.

title: Countable plurals
rule: Countable nouns take a plural form (usually -s/-es). After plural determiners (many, several, two) the noun must be plural.

title: Uncountable nouns
rule: Uncountable nouns (information, advice, furniture, money) have no plural and take a singular verb. Quantify them with 'some', 'much', 'a piece of'.

title: Basic word order: SVO and adverb placement
rule: English default order is Subject-Verb-Object. Adverbs of frequency go before the main verb but after 'be'. Do not place an adverb between verb and object.
examples: I like coffee very much. (not: I like very much coffee)

title: Question word order
rule: Direct questions invert auxiliary and subject (Do you...? Have you...?). Indirect/embedded questions keep statement order (I wonder where he is, not where is he).
examples: Can you tell me where the station is?

title: Modal verbs form
rule: Modals (can, must, should, will) are followed by the bare infinitive with no 'to' and no -s for third person.
examples: She can swim. (not: can to swim / cans swim)

title: First and second conditional
rule: First conditional (real future): if + present, will + base. Second conditional (unreal/hypothetical): if + past, would + base.
examples: If it rains, I will stay home.
If I had money, I would travel.

title: Subject vs object pronouns
rule: Subject pronouns (I, he, she, we, they) do the action; object pronouns (me, him, her, us, them) receive it or follow a preposition.
examples: He and I went out. (not: Me and him)

title: Adjectives are invariable
rule: English adjectives do not change for number or gender and normally come before the noun.

title: Comparatives and superlatives
rule: Short adjectives add -er/-est (bigger, biggest); longer ones use more/most. Use 'than' after a comparative and 'the' before a superlative.

title: Gerund vs infinitive after verbs
rule: Some verbs take a gerund (enjoy, avoid, finish + -ing), others an infinitive (want, decide, hope + to). Learn them per verb.

title: Zero article with generalizations
rule: Do not use 'the' before plural or uncountable nouns when speaking in general. Add 'the' only when referring to specific, identifiable instances.

title: Comma splices and run-on sentences
rule: Do not join two independent clauses with only a comma. Use a full stop, a semicolon, or a coordinating conjunction (and, but, so).
examples: I was tired, so I went to bed.

title: Common confusables
rule: Distinguish its/it's (possessive vs it is), their/there/they're, your/you're, then/than.
examples: It's raining and the dog lost its bone.
You're late; your bag is here.

title: Linking words for cohesion
rule: Use linkers to signal relationships: however (contrast), therefore (result), for example (illustration), in addition (addition). Punctuate them correctly.
examples: The plan failed; however, we learned a lot.

title: Passive voice formation
rule: Form the passive with be + past participle. Use it when the agent is unknown or unimportant. Overuse harms clarity.
examples: The report was written by the team.

title: Relative clauses
rule: Use who for people, which for things, that for both in defining clauses. Do not add a second subject pronoun (the man who he came -> the man who came).
examples: a book that changed my life
